title: Share RV32M ALU datapaths
motivation:
The bench core's EX ALU currently computes three 64-bit products plus
independent signed/unsigned divide and remainder expressions before selecting
one result. Even when CoreMark mostly exercises simple ALU and MUL paths,
those wide arithmetic blocks remain in the EX output cone and add LUT and
routing pressure on the Tang Nano 20K. Reducing redundant arithmetic should
improve median Fmax without adding cycles to the common MUL path.

hypothesis.motivation:

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Rewrite the real RV32M branch of rtl/alu.sv so all multiply variants use one
sign-configurable 33x33 product, and all DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU variants share
normalized unsigned quotient/remainder logic with the existing RISC-V
divide-by-zero and overflow fixups. Keep the RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS substitutions
equivalent and preserve single-cycle ALU latency, so CoreMark IPC should not
regress while the FPGA critical path and LUT/routing pressure improve.

Add or retain focused RV32M corner vectors for MULH/MULHSU/MULHU and DIV/REM divide-by-zero/overflow cases to guard the shared datapath rewrite.

title: Decouple slow RV32M ops from EX
motivation:
The current EX stage routes every instruction through a fully combinational
RV32IM ALU that includes parallel high-half multipliers plus DIV/REM logic.
CoreMark's timed region is dominated by integer ALU, load/store, branch, and
low-half MUL work, while DIV/REM and high-half multiply are either absent or
rare. Keeping those slow operators on the single-cycle EX timing path likely
depresses Fmax without improving CoreMark IPC.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Restructure EX into a fast single-cycle I-ALU plus a separate RV32M slow-unit.
Keep ALU_MUL's low-half multiply on a one-cycle fast path for CoreMark, but
move MULH/MULHSU/MULHU and DIV/REM/REMU into a shared multi-cycle unit that
stalls the in-order pipeline only while those rare operations are in flight.
This should remove the divider and redundant high-multiply logic from the
common EX critical path, reduce LUT pressure near the current budget, and
raise Fmax with minimal impact on the benchmark instruction stream.

title: Static backward-branch next-PC predictor
motivation:
The current five-stage core resolves all branches and jumps in EX, so every
taken loop branch or direct jump pays the redirect flush penalty. CoreMark is
loop-heavy, and a low-cost predictor that fetches backward branch targets and
direct JAL targets immediately should recover cycles without adding large FPGA
structures or touching the single-retire RVFI contract.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current five-stage core resolves all branches and jumps in EX, so every
taken loop branch or direct jump pays the redirect flush penalty. CoreMark is
loop-heavy, and a low-cost predictor that fetches backward branch targets and
direct JAL targets immediately should recover cycles without adding large FPGA
structures or touching the single-retire RVFI contract.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add an IF-stage static next-PC predictor: predict JAL taken, predict
conditional branches with a negative B-immediate taken, and otherwise predict
fall-through. Carry the predicted next PC through IF/ID and ID/EX so EX can
redirect only when the resolved architectural next PC differs from the
prediction. This should turn the common CoreMark loop-back branch from a
taken-branch flush into a correct-path fetch, while forward exits still pay
only the existing mispredict recovery cost.

title: Trim low-half MUL partial products
motivation:
The current best design already removed rare high-half multiply and divide
operations from the common EX result cone, but RV32M low-half MUL remains a
single-cycle 32x32 multiply in the fast ALU. CoreMark benefits from keeping
MUL single-cycle, yet RV32 MUL only needs product bits [31:0], so the high x
high partial product and some upper carry logic are unnecessary FPGA work.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the generic low-half `a * b` implementation in rtl/alu.sv with an
explicit low-32 partial-product decomposition using 16-bit limbs: low x low
plus the lower half of the two cross products shifted by 16. This should let
synthesis drop the unused high x high multiplier work and map the remaining
pieces more cleanly to Tang Nano DSP/LUT resources while preserving the
one-cycle ALU_MUL latency that CoreMark needs. Keep the RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS
substitute unchanged and add focused MUL low-half carry vectors so RV32M
bit-exactness remains covered.

Add MUL low-half edge vectors that exercise cross-product carries and discarded high-half bits.

title: Elastic IF fetch buffer
motivation:
The current front end ties instruction fetch directly to decode: when ID is
stalled for load-use, dmem backpressure, or a slow M operation, the PC also
holds and no useful instruction fetch can be performed. With the tournament
cosim injecting about 22 percent random instruction stalls, those missed
opportunities turn independent stalls into serialized bubbles. The failed
static predictor showed that adding next-PC computation in IF is too costly,
so the better structural target is decoupling fetch from decode without
adding branch-target logic to the IF critical path.

hypothesis.motivation:

stalled for load-use, dmem backpressure, or a slow M operation, the PC also
holds and no useful instruction fetch can be performed. With the tournament

static predictor showed that adding next-PC computation in IF is too costly,
so the better structural target is decoupling fetch from decode without
adding branch-target logic to the IF critical path.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Insert a tiny elastic fetch buffer between IF and ID. The buffer should keep
the existing direct fall-through path when decode is ready, but when ID/EX is
stalled it should let IF continue fetching sequential instructions into one
or two registered slots until the buffer is full. Decode then consumes from
the buffer before requesting a fresh instruction, hiding later imemReady=0
cycles behind earlier load-use, dmem, or slow-M stalls. Redirects flush the
buffer and restart fetch at the resolved target, preserving precise single
retirement and the existing RVFI channel-0 contract.

title: EX-trained tiny BTB predictor
motivation:
The previous static backward-branch predictor recovered some taken-branch
cycles, but it put immediate decode and target addition into the IF next-PC
cone and lost too much Fmax. CoreMark still has hot loop branches and direct
jumps, so the promising predictor direction is to avoid recomputing targets
in IF and instead reuse targets already resolved in EX.

hypothesis.motivation:

cone and lost too much Fmax. CoreMark still has hot loop branches and direct
jumps, so the promising predictor direction is to avoid recomputing targets
in IF and instead reuse targets already resolved in EX.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a small direct-mapped branch target buffer with 2-bit taken counters,
trained when branches and jumps resolve in EX. IF predicts taken only on a
valid tag hit whose counter is taken, then uses the stored target directly;
misses fall through to pc+4. Carry the predicted next PC through ID/EX so EX
redirects only when the resolved architectural next PC differs from the
prediction. This should turn steady-state CoreMark loop-back branches into
correct-path fetches while keeping the IF critical path to a tag compare,
counter bit, target mux, and the existing pc+4 adder rather than the failed
immediate-decode plus target-adder cone.

title: Retiming slow M finalization
motivation:
The current best design already moved high-half multiply and divide/remainder
out of the common EX ALU path, but the multi-cycle m_unit still participates
in FPGA timing. Its final MULH and DIV/REM cycles combine iterative
add/subtract work with sign correction and result selection, creating long
carry/mux cones for operations that CoreMark rarely executes.

hypothesis.motivation:

carry/mux cones for operations that CoreMark rarely executes.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add one explicit finish state to the slow RV32M unit so the last iterative
step captures an unsigned product, quotient, or remainder, and a following
cycle performs any two's-complement sign correction and result muxing. This
increases latency only for rare slow M operations while shortening the
m_unit register-to-register critical paths, preserving the one-cycle
low-half MUL fast path and the CoreMark IPC profile.

lesson: - 2026-05-12 hyp-20260512-001-r3s0 (improvement, +3.22%): Splitting slow RV32M finalization into an unsigned last iteration plus a finish cycle can improve Fmax because sign correction and result selection leave the iterative m_unit carry/mux cone while CoreMark rarely pays the added latency.

title: MEM-to-EX load-use bypass
motivation:
The current five-stage pipeline always inserts a bubble when an instruction
consumes the destination of the immediately preceding load. That is safe but
pessimistic for the zero-wait or ready dmem cycles that dominate the FPGA and
random-stall CoreMark runs: the load value is already formed in MEM while the
dependent instruction would be in EX. Previous frontend structural work lost
too much Fmax, so the better IPC target is a local backend bypass that leaves
IF next-PC timing untouched.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a MEM-stage load-data bypass into the EX operand forwarding network and
change load-use hazard handling so the dependent instruction stalls only when
the load is actually waiting on dmem or cannot produce a valid value. The MEM
stage should expose the sign/zero-extended load result plus a valid rd tag;
EX should select that value ahead of the older MEM/WB path for matching rs1 or
rs2 operands. This should remove common one-cycle load-use bubbles in
CoreMark while preserving the existing single-retire RVFI contract and keeping
branch prediction/front-end timing unchanged.

title: Single-entry loop target predictor
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts recovered some control-flow cycles but lost fitness by
putting too much work in the fetch next-PC path: immediate target calculation
for static prediction, then tag/counter/target array lookup for a tiny BTB.
CoreMark is still loop-heavy, so the best remaining predictor angle is a much
narrower loop-back predictor that only remembers one resolved backward branch
target and avoids any IF instruction decode or target addition.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a single-entry EX-trained backward-loop target latch. When EX resolves a
taken conditional branch whose target is below its PC, record {branch_pc,
branch_target}; when IF later fetches the same PC, predict the stored target.
Clear or mark not-taken when that same branch resolves fall-through so loop
exits recover after one mispredict. Carry predicted-next-PC metadata through
ID/EX so EX suppresses redirects on correct predictions and redirects only on
mismatches. This should save repeated inner-loop taken-branch bubbles with far
less fetch-side cost than the failed static predictor or BTB.

title: Case-based MEM byte lanes
motivation:
The current best core has already moved rare high-half multiply and divide
work out of the common EX path, so the next safest micro-optimization is a
hot load/store datapath cleanup rather than another predictor or bypass.
CoreMark performs many byte, halfword, and word memory accesses, and the MEM
stage currently builds masks with variable shifts and extracts load data with
a variable word shift before sign extension. Even if synthesis simplifies
some of this, the generic shifter/mask form can add LUT depth and routing on
a path that every load reaches.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current best core has already moved rare high-half multiply and divide
work out of the common EX path, so the next safest micro-optimization is a
hot load/store datapath cleanup rather than another predictor or bypass.
CoreMark performs many byte, halfword, and word memory accesses, and the MEM

a path that every load reaches.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Rewrite the MEM-stage byte-lane logic as explicit case statements keyed by
address bits and access width. Generate store masks, replicated write data,
load byte/halfword selection, and RVFI masks directly per lane instead of
using `<<` and `>> (addr[1:0] * 8)` expressions. This preserves the existing
one-cycle MEM latency, dmem handshake behavior, misalignment trapping, and
nret=1 RVFI contract while giving yosys/nextpnr a smaller mux network for the
load-data and mask paths. The expected gain is mostly Fmax with no intentional
CoreMark cycle-count change.

title: Register forwarding selects
motivation: Recent structural IPC attempts added frontend/backend muxing and lost more Fmax than they recovered in cycles. The remaining attractive structural target is the EX operand path itself: live rd/rs compare and priority-select logic currently feeds the ALU and low-half MUL muxes every cycle. Retiming those decisions into the ID/EX register should shorten the hot EX cone without changing branch prediction, load-use behavior, or the nret=1 RVFI contract.

Retiming those decisions into the ID/EX register should shorten the hot EX cone without changing branch prediction, load-use behavior, or the nret=1 RVFI contract.
hypothesis.hypothesis: Compute the forwarding source for the instruction while it is still in decode, then carry two registered forwarding-select fields in id_ex_t. Decode can compare the IF/ID rs addresses against the current ID/EX producer, which will be EX/MEM next cycle, and the current EX/MEM producer, which will be MEM/WB next cycle. The existing load-use bubble remains in place, so a load immediately ahead is not treated as an EX/MEM-forwardable producer. EX then uses only the registered select bits plus the existing forwarded data buses, removing the live comparator/priority network from the ALU and one-cycle MUL timing path while preserving identical IPC and stall semantics.

title: Tiny RAS return predictor
motivation:
Prior branch predictors regressed because they put target adders, tag compares,
or predicted-PC metadata on already-sensitive frontend and EX paths. CoreMark
still executes call/return control flow around its benchmark kernels, and
returns are predictable with much less IF logic than conditional branches or a
BTB: an exact `ret` instruction compare plus a registered return-stack top.

hypothesis.motivation:
Prior branch predictors regressed because they put target adders, tag compares,
or predicted-PC metadata on already-sensitive frontend and EX paths. CoreMark
still executes call/return control flow around its benchmark kernels, and
returns are predictable with much less IF logic than conditional branches or a
BTB: an exact `ret` instruction compare plus a registered return-stack top.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a tiny return-address-stack predictor for canonical RISC-V returns only.
Train the stack when EX resolves link-register JAL/JALR calls, and when IF
fetches `jalr x0, 0(x1)` or `jalr x0, 0(x5)` with a valid stack entry, use the
stored top as the next PC. Carry only return-prediction metadata to EX so a
correctly predicted return suppresses the normal JALR redirect, while a bad or
empty-stack prediction redirects to the architecturally resolved target. This
should recover return redirect bubbles with far less timing cost than the
failed static branch, BTB, and loop-target predictors because IF does not
compute branch immediates, add targets, or compare fetch PCs against tags.

title: Source-aware load-use stalls
motivation:
The current hazard unit treats both raw rs1 and rs2 bitfields as live source
operands for every instruction. That is conservative, but it can insert a
one-cycle load-use bubble when the following instruction is LUI, AUIPC, JAL,
EBREAK/FENCE, or an I-type instruction whose immediate bits merely alias the
previous load destination. CoreMark contains many load/immediate/control-flow
sequences, so avoiding these false bubbles should improve cycles without
adding a bypass path or fetch-side predictor logic.

hypothesis.motivation:

sequences, so avoiding these false bubbles should improve cycles without

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add small source-usage qualification for load-use detection and decode-time
forwarding select generation. The hazard unit should only compare a pending
load destination against rs1 when the IF/ID instruction architecturally reads
rs1, and only against rs2 when it architecturally reads rs2. Keep true
load-use bubbles for OP, BRANCH, STORE, LOAD/OP-IMM/JALR rs1 dependencies,
preserve the existing no-MEM-bypass policy, and leave RVFI retirement and
trap behavior unchanged. This is a localized IPC micro-optimization with far
less timing risk than the failed MEM-to-EX load bypass.

title: Register WB forwarding data
motivation:
The r4 structural win registered forwarding selects, which suggests the EX
operand cone is still timing-sensitive after the slow RV32M work was moved
off the common path. MEM/WB forwarding still feeds EX through the live WB
write-data mux, so a dependent instruction forwarded from WB carries
mem_to_reg selection into the EX operand mux, ALU, and one-cycle MUL path.
That mux is small, but it sits on the hottest every-cycle datapath and does
not buy CoreMark any IPC.

title: Decode-stage JAL target predictor
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts lost fitness because they put branch immediate decode,
tag compares, target arrays, or RAS logic directly in the fetch next-PC path.
CoreMark still contains direct calls and unconditional jumps, and those are
deterministic once the instruction is visible in decode. A narrower predictor
can recover some redirect bubbles without adding conditional branch direction
state or a BTB-style lookup to every fetch.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add an early direct-JAL target predictor in decode: when ID sees an aligned
JAL instruction, compute pc+J_imm using the existing decoded immediate path
and redirect IF one stage earlier than the normal EX redirect. Carry a compact
predicted-direct-jump bit and predicted target through ID/EX so EX suppresses
the ordinary JAL redirect when the prediction matches, and recovers normally
for misaligned targets or any mismatch. This focuses the predictor on
unconditional direct control flow, avoiding the failed conditional-branch,
loop-BTB, and RAS structures while still saving bubbles on CoreMark calls and
direct jumps.

title: Precompute PC-relative EX values
motivation:
The current best results indicate that fitness gains are coming from Fmax,
not from added prediction or bypass IPC. The EX stage still recomputes
pc+4 and pc+imm for branch, JAL, JALR link, and AUIPC handling, and it keeps
an is_auipc mux in front of the fast ALU operand path. Those deterministic
PC-relative values can be registered before EX instead of sitting in the
ALU/MUL and redirect timing cones.

remains in EX because it depends on the forwarded rs1 operand. This should
remove PC adders and the AUIPC operand mux from hot EX paths without changing
pipeline depth, stall policy, RVFI ordering, or CoreMark cycle count.

title: Slim unused pipeline sidebands
motivation:
Recent structural wins came from shortening hot pipeline cones without adding
new IPC machinery, while fetch buffers, load bypasses, and predictors lost
more Fmax than they recovered. The current RTL still carries some metadata
that is not consumed after EX, including EX/MEM branch_taken and
branch_target fields plus an is_lui control bit that is redundant with
ALU_LUI. Those dead sidebands add registers, mux fanout, and routing pressure
on a design where small retiming changes have already moved fitness.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Prune the unused pipeline sidebands rather than changing execution behavior:
remove branch_taken and branch_target from ex_mem_t and stop registering them,
and remove the dead is_lui control bit from ctrl_t while keeping LUI legality
represented by ALU_LUI and the existing immediate path. Branch redirect and
pc_next calculation still use local EX signals in the same cycle, so IPC,
stall behavior, traps, and the nret=1 RVFI contract are unchanged. The smaller
pipeline payload should reduce EX/MEM fanout, backend register count, and
nextpnr routing pressure, yielding a modest Fmax-driven fitness gain.

title: Countdown loop predictor
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts recovered possible control-flow cycles but lost more
FPGA fitness through fetch-side instruction decode, target adders, BTB/RAS
lookup, or exact PC compare paths. CoreMark is still loop-heavy, so the most
promising remaining predictor angle is to keep loop prediction state cheap
enough that it does not dominate the IF next-PC cone.

hypothesis.motivation:
Prior predictor attempts recovered possible control-flow cycles but lost more
FPGA fitness through fetch-side instruction decode, target adders, BTB/RAS
lookup, or exact PC compare paths. CoreMark is still loop-heavy, so the most
promising remaining predictor angle is to keep loop prediction state cheap
enough that it does not dominate the IF next-PC cone.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a single-entry backward-loop countdown predictor trained by EX when a
conditional branch resolves taken to a lower PC. Store the loop target and
the branch-to-target distance in words, then count sequential fetch advances
from the target; when the counter reaches zero, predict the stored loop
target without decoding the fetched instruction or comparing the fetch PC
against a tag. Carry compact prediction metadata through ID/EX so EX can
suppress the normal taken-branch redirect on a correct loop prediction and
recover to pc+4 on the loop exit or any mismatch. This should preserve most
of the useful inner-loop benefit of the failed loop/BTB predictors while
replacing the IF exact-PC/tag compare with a small zero-count check.

title: Share branch compare flags
motivation:
Recent successful bench-core changes improved fitness by shortening hot FPGA
timing paths without adding new prediction, buffering, or bypass machinery.
The EX stage still builds branch decisions with separate equality, signed,
and unsigned comparisons selected by branch_op. CoreMark is branch-heavy, and
a taken branch redirect path must resolve in the same cycle as the ALU path.

hypothesis.motivation:

timing paths without adding new prediction, buffering, or bypass machinery.
The EX stage still builds branch decisions with separate equality, signed,
and unsigned comparisons selected by branch_op. CoreMark is branch-heavy, and
a taken branch redirect path must resolve in the same cycle as the ALU path.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the per-branch comparison expressions in rtl/ex_stage.sv with shared
compare flags: one equality check, one unsigned less-than flag, and a small
sign-bit correction for signed less-than. BEQ/BNE and BGE/BGEU can use these
flags and inversions instead of independent signed/unsigned comparator cones.
This keeps the five-stage pipeline, redirect timing, load-use policy, RVFI
ordering, and CoreMark cycle count unchanged while giving synthesis a smaller
EX branch-condition mux network and potentially improving Fmax.

title: LUTRAM-backed unreset regfile
motivation:
Recent structural attempts that added frontend prediction, fetch buffering,
MEM bypassing, or extra WB state regressed because they added muxing and
control to already sensitive paths. The current register file is still a
32x32 reset-cleared, two-read flop array with write-first bypass muxes; that
reset loop and wide read muxing consume many FFs and routing resources even
though RV32I only requires x0 to be zero after reset. Removing the
non-architectural full-register reset is a localized structural change with
no intended CoreMark cycle-count impact.

hypothesis.motivation:

control to already sensitive paths. The current register file is still a

though RV32I only requires x0 to be zero after reset. Removing the
non-architectural full-register reset is a localized structural change with
no intended CoreMark cycle-count impact.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Recode rtl/reg_file.sv as an unreset, duplicated two-read-bank register file
suitable for distributed LUT RAM inference. Keep both banks written together,
keep x0 hardwired to zero, and retain the explicit same-cycle write-first
bypass so the existing decode/forwarding contract is unchanged. With reset
removed from the 31 writable registers, yosys/nextpnr should have less reset
fanout, fewer flop-based read muxes, and better packing/routing around the
decode-to-ID/EX path, improving Fmax without changing IPC, RVFI ordering, or
any hard invariant.

title: Registered lookahead loop predictor
motivation:
CoreMark is still loop-heavy, so the remaining control-flow opportunity is
reducing repeated taken backward-branch redirects. The prior predictor
attempts show that the useful predictor must not put instruction decode,
target adders, BTB tag compares, or RAS reads on the same-cycle IF next-PC
path, because Fmax loss dominates the saved bubbles.

hypothesis.motivation:
CoreMark is still loop-heavy, so the remaining control-flow opportunity is
reducing repeated taken backward-branch redirects. The prior predictor
attempts show that the useful predictor must not put instruction decode,
target adders, BTB tag compares, or RAS reads on the same-cycle IF next-PC
path, because Fmax loss dominates the saved bubbles.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a tiny EX-trained backward-loop predictor whose lookup is performed one
fetch ahead for the next sequential PC and latched before it can affect the
PC update. While fetching PC P, IF compares the trained loop branch PC
against P+4 and captures a registered hit/target for the instruction that
will be fetched next; when that next instruction is actually at the
loop-branch PC, the PC update selects the already-registered target with no
live tag compare or target-array read in the PC feedback cone. EX trains the
entry on taken backward conditional branches and invalidates it when the same
branch resolves not taken, while carried prediction metadata lets EX suppress
correct predicted redirects or recover normally on loop exit. This keeps the
predictor focused on hot loop-back branches and directly addresses the timing
failure mode of the earlier static, BTB, RAS, and countdown predictors.

title: Narrow slow MULH adder
motivation:
The best prior micro-optimizations improved fitness by shortening FPGA timing
cones without adding frontend prediction or load bypass control. The slow
RV32M unit is still synthesized into the design, and its high-half multiply
loop currently uses a 64-bit accumulator plus a shifted 64-bit multiplicand,
creating a wide carry path for operations that CoreMark rarely executes.

hypothesis.motivation:
The best prior micro-optimizations improved fitness by shortening FPGA timing
cones without adding frontend prediction or load bypass control. The slow
RV32M unit is still synthesized into the design, and its high-half multiply
loop currently uses a 64-bit accumulator plus a shifted 64-bit multiplicand,
creating a wide carry path for operations that CoreMark rarely executes.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the slow MULH/MULHU/MULHSU iterative multiply datapath in m_unit.sv
with a right-shift partial-product algorithm that adds the 32-bit magnitude
operand into the upper half of a combined product register. This keeps the
existing multi-cycle behavior and separate finish-state sign correction, but
reduces the per-cycle multiply carry path from roughly 64 bits to about 33
bits. CoreMark should see nearly unchanged cycle count because low-half MUL
remains single-cycle in the fast ALU and slow high-half multiplies are rare,
while median FPGA Fmax may improve from the narrower m_unit timing cone.

title: Registered low-half MUL path
motivation:
Prior structural wins came from taking rare RV32M work out of the common EX
result cone, while frontend predictors, fetch buffering, MEM bypassing, and
regfile rewrites all lost more timing than they recovered. The remaining
high-cost logic still present on every fast EX timing analysis path is the
single-cycle 32x32 low-half MUL in alu.sv. CoreMark needs MUL support, but it
does not execute MUL every cycle, so a small MUL-only latency penalty may be
cheaper than keeping the multiplier in the ADD/load-address/branch ALU cone.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Split ALU_MUL out of the combinational fast ALU and handle it with a tiny
registered low-half multiply side path in EX. Non-MUL RV32I ALU operations,
branch compares, load/store address generation, JAL/JALR link values, and the
existing slow high-half/DIV/REM m_unit behavior should remain unchanged. When
ALU_MUL reaches EX, the pipeline holds for the registered product, then emits
exactly one EX/MEM result with the original RVFI operands and order. Under
RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS the side path should register the same formal substitute
expression instead of the real product. This should remove the DSP/multiplier
path from the common ALU output mux and improve Tang Nano Fmax enough to
offset occasional MUL bubbles in CoreMark.

title: JAL-only target cache
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts show that CoreMark does not repay broad IF-stage
branch machinery: static backward prediction, BTBs, loop predictors, and RAS
variants all lost Fmax through target adders, tag/counter lookup, recovery
metadata, or extra next-PC muxing. The remaining control-flow predictor angle
with a plausible timing budget is to predict only direct JAL instructions,
which are unconditionally taken and do not need direction counters or branch
condition state.

resolved target in a two-entry cache; when IF fetches a JAL opcode with a

metadata to EX. EX suppresses the normal redirect when the stored target

redirect/trap path. This avoids the failed static predictor's IF immediate

title: Isolate fast EX result mux
motivation:
The current best result came from registering low-half MUL so the 32x32
multiplier no longer sits in the common ALU cone. However, normal ADD,
load-address, branch-adjacent, and jump-link results still feed the EX/MEM
register through a shared result select that includes low-half MUL and the
slow M-unit result. CoreMark executes M operations sparsely, so keeping
those M-result inputs on the every-cycle fast EX register input may cost
Fmax without improving IPC.

side-unit handshakes, two result-valid paths, and a three-way result select for
logic that is only active on M-extension instructions.

title: Stall-shadow branch predictor
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts on this core lost fitness because they put target
adders, tag compares, counters, return-stack reads, or prediction recovery
muxes on the normal IF next-PC path. The random-stall CoreMark methodology
still creates cycles where IF is holding a valid instruction while decode,
dmem, or a slow M operation is stalled. Those otherwise idle hold cycles can
be used to prepare a prediction without making every fetch pay the full
predictor timing cost.

stalled on a valid held instruction. During the hold window, predecode only
aligned direct JALs and negative-immediate conditional branches, compute the

already-registered target and carries compact predicted-next-PC metadata to
EX. EX suppresses the normal redirect when the resolved next PC matches the
shadow prediction and recovers through the existing flush path on mismatch or
loop exit. This targets control-flow bubbles that immediately follow existing
load-use, dmem, imem, or slow-M stalls while avoiding live predictor lookup,
immediate decode, and target addition on ordinary fall-through fetch cycles.

prediction, bypass, or frontend machinery. The current RTL still resets large
datapath payloads in ID/EX, EX/MEM, MEM/WB, and the RV32M side units even
though only valid/control bits are required to suppress retirement, writes,
redirects, and memory side effects after reset or flush.

title: Posted store buffer
motivation:
The current pipeline freezes all stages whenever a store reaches MEM while
io_dmemReady is low, even though the store has no register result and many
following CoreMark instructions are independent integer work. Prior
structural attempts that added frontend prediction or load-use bypassing
paid too much timing cost, but store-side decoupling can target dStall IPC
loss with only local MEM/hazard changes and no IF next-PC cone growth.

EX/MEM and dmem is not ready, capture its address, replicated write data,
mask, and RVFI memory fields, allow the store to retire once into MEM/WB,

the external dmem port. Stall subsequent loads or stores until the buffered
store has been accepted, preserving in-order memory semantics without a
load-forwarding network. This should hide a meaningful fraction of random
store dStall cycles in CoreMark while keeping the nret=1 RVFI retirement
contract unchanged and avoiding the frontend timing penalties that hurt
earlier predictor/buffer experiments.

title: Exact trivial-MUL result predictor
motivation:
Prior CoreMark branch predictors repeatedly lost fitness by adding lookup,
target, mux, and recovery logic to the IF next-PC path. The current best
core instead gained by registering low-half MUL, but that makes every MUL
pay an EX hold even when the result is an obvious function of a zero, one,
or minus-one operand. A predictor that classifies only those exact operand
cases can recover some MUL bubbles without touching fetch prediction.

title: Share EX PC adders
motivation:
The best bench-core results have come from small Fmax-oriented changes that
keep the pipeline shape unchanged and avoid new prediction, bypass, or
exception fast paths. The current EX stage still recomputes pc+imm for both
branch/JAL target handling and AUIPC, recomputes pc+4 for link and fall-through
paths, and places a rare AUIPC-controlled 32-bit mux in front of the fast ALU
operand path used by ordinary ADD, load/store address generation, and branch
work. The prior PC-precompute attempt regressed because it added ID-side
adders and wider ID/EX state; this narrower cleanup keeps the adders local to
EX and only removes duplicated source expressions and a hot input mux.

title: Split WB forwarding shadow
motivation:
Prior structural wins came from removing hot-path logic without adding new
frontend prediction or memory-order machinery. The current MEM/WB register
retains every RVFI and datapath field during a dmem stall solely so a held
dependent instruction can keep seeing the previous WB forwarding value. That
makes hold_mem_wb fan out across a wide retirement bundle even though only
the WB forwarding data must survive the stall.

title: Redirect target replay cache
motivation:
Every prior control-flow predictor on this core lost fitness because it put
lookup, target-add, next-PC muxing, or prediction metadata on the normal
fetch path. The existing pipeline still pays a redirect bubble for hot loop
branches and direct jumps after EX has already resolved the correct target.
A predictor that only acts on resolved redirects can target those bubbles
without perturbing the common fall-through `pc + 4` fetch path.

asserts a redirect to a cached target, IF injects the cached target
instruction into ID in that same cycle and advances the PC to the cached

would have killed this injected instruction. Cache misses keep the existing
redirect behavior exactly. This is a predictor of the target instruction,
not a branch-direction predictor: there is no EX redirect suppression, no
speculative wrong-path retirement, and no predictor lookup on normal
fall-through fetches. Hot CoreMark loop targets should hit after warmup and
save one redirect bubble with much lower timing risk than the failed IF-side
branch, BTB, RAS, loop, JAL, and stall-shadow predictors.

reintroducing multiplier logic into EX, folding MUL into the shared M unit,
or adding operand-class bypasses regresses QoR. A narrower MUL-only internal
datapath keeps the successful isolation while attacking the remaining
multiplier timing path.

or adding predictor, memory, or bypass machinery that has regressed before.

results feed the EX stall, start, ready, and result-select cones. A small
ID-stage retime can shorten that control path without changing CoreMark IPC
or adding predictor, memory, or bypass machinery that has regressed before.

result_ready, operand latching, and the M-result select, while preserving
the separate one-cycle mul_unit and the existing multi-cycle m_unit. This
follows the proven pattern of moving small compare/priority decisions out
of the EX cone, avoids reconnecting MUL to the slow shared unit, and should
provide a modest Fmax-driven fitness gain with no intentional cycle-count

title: Decode return target predictor
motivation:
Prior predictor experiments on this core repeatedly lost Fmax by putting
branch target adders, tag compares, BTB/RAS reads, or replay muxing on the
fetch next-PC path. The least costly remaining control-flow opportunity is
canonical function returns: CoreMark still has call/return structure, and a
return target is already available as the decoded rs1 register value for
well-spaced `jalr x0, 0(x1/x5)` instructions.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a decode-stage exact return target predictor for canonical returns only.

ID is not stalled/flushed, the rs1 value does not need EX/MEM forwarding, and
the cleared JALR target is word-aligned, redirect IF to that register target
one cycle before the existing EX JALR redirect. Carry a compact predicted-ret
bit and target in ID/EX so EX suppresses the normal redirect only when the
resolved target matches; all forwarded, misaligned, or mismatching cases fall
back to the current EX redirect/trap behavior. This targets return bubbles
with much less timing risk than the failed IF-side RAS, BTB, JAL, loop, and
replay predictors because there is no fetch-stage instruction decode, target
addition, stack lookup, or tag compare on the common fall-through path.

title: Input-registered low MUL
motivation:
The best current bench result came from isolating low-half MUL from the
common EX ALU cone, while later attempts showed that partial-product MUL,
shared M-unit dispatch, and trivial operand bypasses add too much control or
arithmetic structure. The remaining MUL-specific timing risk is the direct
forwarded-operand-to-32x32-product path inside the standalone mul_unit.

hypothesis.hypothesis:

preserve the successful single inferred 32x32 product while shortening its
input cone. On start, mul_unit would latch operands and mark itself busy;
the following cycle computes and registers the product from those stable
operands, then presents the existing result_valid/result_ready handshake to
EX. This adds one extra cycle only to sparse CoreMark MUL instructions, avoids
reintroducing multiplier logic into the fast ALU path, and avoids the failed
16x16 partial-product decomposition.

title: Segment RVFI order counter
motivation:
The FPGA Fmax wrapper deliberately XOR-reduces all RVFI outputs, so the
retirement sidecar remains live in timing even though it does not affect
CoreMark execution. The current core increments rvfi_order with a single
64-bit +1 operation on every retirement, creating a wide carry path in a
design where recent useful gains came from removing or retiming hot cones
without adding prediction, buffering, or bypass machinery.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the monolithic rvfi_order_q <= rvfi_order_q + 64'd1 update with an
exact segmented retirement-order generator, for example four 16-bit limbs
or two 32-bit halves with explicit carry enables. The channel-0 RVFI output
still presents the full concatenated 64-bit order before increment, reset
still starts at zero, and each valid retirement still advances the order by
exactly one with no gaps or duplicates. This should shorten the retirement
counter register-to-register path and reduce RVFI-side routing pressure in
the Fmax wrapper while leaving the pipeline, CoreMark cycle count, nret=1
port contract, trap behavior, and memory semantics unchanged.

title: Speculative EX branch direction
motivation:
Every prior frontend predictor for this core has lost more FPGA timing than
it recovered in CoreMark cycles because lookup, target generation, or
prediction metadata landed on the IF next-PC path. The remaining predictor
opportunity is to keep fetch as simple fall-through-plus-redirect while
shortening the EX redirect decision itself.

hypothesis.motivation:

prediction metadata landed on the IF next-PC path. The remaining predictor

shortening the EX redirect decision itself.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Predict conditional branch direction in EX with the cheap static heuristic
already implied by CoreMark loop structure: backward branches predict taken,
forward branches predict not taken. Use that prediction to drive the EX
redirect path without waiting for the full rs1/rs2 branch comparator, then
verify the actual branch condition one stage later in MEM and issue an older
late redirect on mismatch. Correct backward-loop iterations keep the current
redirect timing, loop exits pay a one-cycle-later recovery, and the normal IF
fall-through path gains no BTB, RAS, target-cache, or instruction-predecode
logic. If the branch comparator/control fanout is part of the current Tang
Nano critical path, this should trade a small number of extra loop-exit
bubbles for a modest Fmax gain.

title: Drop regfile reset fanout
motivation:
Recent micro_opt attempts show that added forwarding, predictor, and result
mux control usually costs more Fmax than it saves in CoreMark cycles. The
register file still has a large synchronous reset loop over all 32 x 32 bits,
even though RV32IM only architecturally requires x0 to read as zero and the
benchmark startup code initializes the live registers it uses.

benchmark startup code initializes the live registers it uses.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Remove the reset clearing loop from rtl/reg_file.sv while preserving the
existing single-array storage shape, x0 hardwire behavior, and same-cycle
write-first bypass. This is narrower than the failed duplicated LUTRAM
regfile rewrite: it does not add read-bank replication, write fanout, or new
decode muxing. Dropping the non-architectural GPR reset should remove roughly
one thousand reset mux/load points and may let synthesis pack or route the
regfile more efficiently, improving Fmax without changing IPC, RVFI order,
memory behavior, or the nret=1 top-level contract.

title: Registered RVFI sidecar
motivation:
The FPGA benchmark wrapper keeps the RVFI outputs live by reducing them, so
retirement metadata can still influence Fmax even though it does not drive
execution. Prior structural wins came from removing load from hot EX/RV32M
cones, while prediction, buffering, MEM bypassing, store buffering, and
regfile rewrites repeatedly lost more timing than they recovered. A narrow
remaining structural target is the direct fanout from MEM/WB and the live WB
result mux into the wide RVFI output bus.

title: Exact redirect target injector
motivation:
The predictor history for this core is dominated by regressions from putting
speculative lookup, target generation, or recovery metadata on the normal
fetch next-PC path. However, CoreMark still pays repeated bubbles on resolved
taken loop branches and unconditional no-link jumps. A narrower predictor-side
opportunity is to use the exact EX redirect target only after the direction
and target are already known, avoiding BTBs, counters, RAS state, and IF-side
immediate decoding.

title: Retimed divider iteration
motivation:
The best bench-core gains have come from removing or retiming RV32M logic
that affects FPGA Fmax while CoreMark rarely pays the added slow-operation
latency. The remaining real DIV/REM loop in rtl/m_unit.sv still combines a
33-bit shifted-remainder compare, conditional subtract, quotient update, and
remainder feedback in one cycle. That path is independent of the already
isolated low-half MUL fast path and is a plausible residual timing limiter.

hypothesis.motivation:

latency. The remaining real DIV/REM loop in rtl/m_unit.sv still combines a

remainder feedback in one cycle. That path is independent of the already

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Split the real-hardware divider loop into two shorter registered phases per
quotient bit: one phase forms the shifted remainder and comparison decision,
and the next phase applies the conditional subtract and updates quotient,
dividend, and remainder state. Keep divide-by-zero and signed-overflow
shortcuts unchanged, keep the ALTOPS formal branch unchanged, and preserve
the existing result_valid/result_ready contract. DIV/REM latency increases,
but CoreMark should execute those operations rarely enough that any cycle
cost is outweighed by a modest Tang Nano Fmax improvement.

title: Direct-mapped IF hit cache
motivation:
Prior structural attempts show that this core benefits most when a change
removes repeated hot-path stalls without adding branch prediction or broad
bypass machinery. The failed elastic IF buffer could only overlap fetch
with other stalls, so it did not directly eliminate the tournament's random
imemReady bubbles. CoreMark repeatedly executes a small set of hot loop
instruction PCs, making repeated instruction fetch stalls a promising target.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a small direct-mapped instruction hit cache inside the IF stage. On a
tag-valid hit for the current PC, IF supplies the cached instruction and
treats fetch as ready even when the external io_imemReady is low; on a miss,
it preserves the current behavior, waits for io_imemReady, fills the entry,
and then advances. Redirects keep the existing EX-resolved flush behavior,
with no branch predictor, no target lookup, and no suppression metadata.

while keeping the nret=1 RVFI contract, retirement ordering, traps, and
dmem semantics unchanged.

title: Accept-gated redirect injector
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts on this core repeatedly lost fitness because they put
target adders, tag compares, counters, RAS reads, or prediction metadata on
the normal fall-through fetch path. The recent exact redirect-target injector
avoided that predictor lookup cost but broke because it could advance the PC
to target+4 while the injected target instruction was still bubbled by ID
flush or accept logic. A corrected version that only injects when decode will
actually capture the target keeps the attractive timing profile while fixing
the known correctness hazard.

avoided that predictor lookup cost but broke because it could advance the PC

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add an accept-gated, resolved-target injection path for already-known taken
control transfers, focused on conditional branches and no-link direct JALs.
When EX asserts a redirect, imem is ready, the redirecting instruction has no
link/writeback dependency, and hazard logic proves ID will accept a real
instruction this cycle, IF temporarily addresses the resolved target, presents
that target instruction to decode, and advances the PC to target+4. In every
other case, including any ID hold, load-use bubble, dmem/slow-M stall, imem

bubble-and-fetch redirect behavior. This is a predictor-side target replay of
an exact resolved redirect, not a speculative branch-direction lookup, so it
should save one bubble on hot CoreMark loop backs without adding work to the
common fall-through next-PC path.
